one_line_takeaway: A multi-modal (OCR + video-to-speech-to-text + LSTM) layered pipeline aimed at phishing that hides in images/deep-fake video; conceptually addresses a real detector blind spot but is validated only on the authors' own synthetic sites with a self-reported 100% and no comparative or false-positive evaluation.
key_finding: On the authors' own 4 self-constructed phishing scenarios the framework reports 100% detection accuracy, with each layer catching content the prior layers would miss (text-in-image caught at Layer 2, video caught at Layer 3, friendly-URL/hacked-DNS text passing Layer 1 but caught by Layer 4). Component metrics: the Layer-1 Random Forest reaches ~90.1% accuracy (Table I, max_depth=30, random_state=0; accuracy is essentially flat, 88.1-90.1%, across depth/random-state settings), and the Layer-4 LSTM reaches 0.98 accuracy at 0.08 loss on the spam dataset. The headline claim is qualitative adaptability (detecting multi-modal/deep-fake phishing that text/URL-only detectors cannot), not a benchmarked accuracy gain over prior systems.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a 4-layer 'adaptive' phishing-detection framework whose stated novelty is detecting phishing content that hides itself from text/URL-only detectors by embedding it in images, in deep-fake videos, or behind SEO-friendly URLs on compromised/hacked-DNS domains. Layer 1 does traditional URL-feature classification with Random Forest; Layer 2 web-scrapes the page HTML behind the scenes, extracts image paths, and uses OCR (computer vision) to convert image text to raw text; Layer 3 extracts video paths, converts video->audio (.wav) via gtts/pydub/moviepy, and speech-synthesizes/recognizes the audio into raw text; Layer 4 merges the text from all prior layers and makes a final prediction with an LSTM (an RNN variant). The claimed contribution is the multi-modal pipeline (vision + speech-to-text + NLP layered together), NOT a new classifier algorithm.
threat_model: Defender is an automated anti-phishing detector; adversary is a phisher who deliberately evades text/URL-based ML detectors. Concrete evasions the paper targets: (1) putting attack text inside images so OCR-less detectors see no malicious text; (2) delivering the lure via deep-fake AI-generated video; (3) 'friendly'/SEO URLs that lack the lexical features (#, dots, URL length, IP, domain levels) classifiers key on; (4) hosting on a hacked/compromised legitimate DNS so domain-age/PageRank/WHOIS features misclassify it as benign; (5) blacklist/whitelist evasion via minor URL edits and the lack of a centralized blacklist. The paper frames these as the reasons current detectors are vulnerable and positions the multi-modal layers as the countermeasure. Note: the framework is detection-only; the paper does not evaluate an adversary attacking the framework itself (e.g., adversarial perturbation of the OCR/ASR/LSTM stages).
limitations: The 100% figure is measured only on a handful of phishing sites the authors built and hosted themselves, with no legitimate sites in that adaptability test, so it reports zero false-positive information and essentially confirms the pipeline can re-read content it authored. Layers 1 and 4 are trained/evaluated on two separate datasets (URL features vs. spam text) rather than end-to-end on multi-modal phishing, so the reported component accuracies are not on the same task as the 100% claim. There is a dataset-size inconsistency (Kaggle described as 5,157 unique records, but the LSTM is trained on 5,572 samples). The multi-modal (image/video/deep-fake) data is internally simulated and the video/audio portion is not released 'for privacy,' limiting reproducibility of the very capability the paper is about. No head-to-head comparison against any prior system is actually run; prior accuracies are only quoted from the literature. The paper acknowledges computational cost (Random Forest tree-building, LSTM training) and server response time as open issues. Adversarial robustness of the OCR/ASR/LSTM stages is not evaluated.

datasets_used: Mendeley phishing dataset, 'B' version (11,430 URLs, 87 extracted features; 56 URL structure/syntax features, 24 page-content features, 7 external-service features; 50% phishing / 50% legitimate) - used at Layer 1 (Random Forest)
Kaggle Spam Message Classification dataset (stated 5,157 unique records; LSTM trained on 5,572 samples with 10,000 max features / 9,004 unique words) - used at Layer 4 (LSTM)
Internally simulated / generated deep-fake videos and text-in-image data (no public multi-modal phishing dataset available; video/audio not fully released for privacy)

4 author-constructed live phishing sites (text-only on friendly URL + hacked DNS, image-only, video-only, all-features) hosted on a secure server with compromised DNS - used for end-to-end adaptability validation
quantitative_results: Framework end-to-end: 100% detection accuracy across each of the 4 self-created phishing scenarios
Layer-1 Random Forest: 90.1% accuracy at max_depth=30/random_state=0 (Table I range 88.1%-90.1% across depth and random-state settings; described as negligible variation)
Layer-4 LSTM: 0.98 accuracy and 0.08 loss at optimal parameters (dense layer=1, sigmoid, epochs=10, batch size=60, feature size=32)
Feature selection at Layer 1: chi2 reduced 87 features to best 19 (k-value=7); 80/20 train/validation split
Layer-4 LSTM data split: 80% of 5,572 samples for training, 20% for validation
baselines_compared: No direct head-to-head baseline experiment is run; the framework is not benchmarked against prior systems on shared data.

Prior accuracies are only cited from Related Work as context (NOT reproduced by the authors), e.g.: Yaswanth & Nagaraju 95% Naive Bayes / 94.67% Random Forest; Abdul Karim et al. hybrid 98.2%; Ishwarya et al. 98.2% Naive Bayes (on an imbalanced 87% ham / 13% spam set); Ann Zeky et al. 99.1% (balanced 7,000-record set); Nishitha et al. Logistic Regression 95% / CNN 96%; Twana & Murat best Naive Bayes 93.39%.

Internal design choice justified rather than compared: Random Forest chosen at Layer 1 and LSTM at Layer 4 (LSTM cited as solving RNN vanishing/exploding-gradient issues); no ablation swapping these components.
